Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Farzin Ratan Karma

Facts.Text: Around 2003, Farzin Ratan Karma met Helen Campos while he was Regional Technical Director of Ulysess System (Singapore) Pte Ltd, a computer software and management company that was being advised by a law firm where she worked.
In 2003, Farzin Ratan Karma held the position of Regional Technical Director at Ulysess System (Singapore) Pte Ltd.

Around October 2006, after ELTICI Corporate Services Pte Ltd decided to sell its corporate secretarial practice and Helen Campos decided to leave, Farzin Ratan Karma provided support and assistance to her in connection with her departure and the setting up of a new business, including logistics, dealing with movers and contractors, and setting up an IT network system.
On 2006-12-12, Farzin Ratan Karma was appointed a director of MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd.
On 2007-06-09, Farzin Ratan Karma was appointed as a signatory for MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd's bank account with United Overseas Bank Limited.
On 2008-02-19, Farzin Ratan Karma acquired 35,000 shares in MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd, representing a 35% shareholding.

By 2008, Farzin Ratan Karma provided at least minimal contributions to the starting up and running of MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd and MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd, including secondary matters such as logistics and IT setup.
On 2009-04-21, Farzin Ratan Karma was appointed a director of MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd.
On 2009-04-21, Farzin Ratan Karma acquired 805 shares in MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd, representing a 35% shareholding.
On 2009-04-23, Farzin Ratan Karma ceased to hold his 35% shareholding in MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd and ceased to be a director of that company.
On 2010-09-17, Farzin Ratan Karma regained a 35% shareholding in MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd amounting to 805 shares.
On 2013-02-25, Farzin Ratan Karma was appointed again as a director of MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd.
On 2013-01-18, Farzin Ratan Karma incorporated Farohar Enterprizes, a management consultancy company, and became its sole director and shareholder.
In 2013, Farzin Ratan Karma left his employment with Ulysess System (Singapore) Pte Ltd.

A Directors' Agreement dated 2014-01-01 was prepared to govern the relationship between Farzin Ratan Karma and Helen Campos and their financial entitlements in relation to MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd, MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd, HC Consultancy Pte Ltd, and Farohar Enterprizes.

On 2014-04-23, Farzin Ratan Karma and Helen Campos signed the Directors' Agreement, which included terms on remuneration, a lump sum payment of S$350,000 from Helen Campos to Farzin Ratan Karma, and apportionment of billings via HC Consultancy Pte Ltd and Farohar Enterprizes.

On 2014-04-23, under the Directors' Agreement, Helen Campos agreed to pay S$350,000 to Farzin Ratan Karma in full and final settlement of possible outstanding business matters including salary, consultancy fees, claims, and dividends among the MC group for the period January 2007 to December 2013.
As of 2017-11-02, the accounts of MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd and MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd recorded that Farzin Ratan Karma owed a total of S$546,015.76 to those companies.

On 2017-11-02, Farzin Ratan Karma and Helen Campos entered into a Waiver Agreement under which the recorded debt of S$546,015.76 owed by him to MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd and MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd was waived.

On 2020-01-10, MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd and Farohar Enterprizes entered into Rose's Salary Agreement, under which an employee of MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd, Asis Rose Dela Rosa, would spend part of her working hours providing services to Farohar Enterprizes in exchange for a monthly payment of S$1,700 from Farohar Enterprizes to MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd.

Under Rose's Salary Agreement dated 2020-01-10, Farohar Enterprizes was to pay MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd S$1,700 per month for services rendered by employee Asis Rose Dela Rosa, with the arrangement terminable on one month's written notice or when she was no longer on assignment with Farohar Enterprizes.
Up to 2020-12-01, Farohar Enterprizes made monthly payments of S$1,700 to MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd under Rose's Salary Agreement.
From 2021-01-01 to 2021-07-01, Farohar Enterprizes did not pay the monthly S$1,700 amounts to MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd under Rose's Salary Agreement.

On 2019-02-28, Farzin Ratan Karma sent an email to Helen Campos stating that she had booked S$360,000 as her salary without supporting documentation and approval from the board and without an employment agreement signed by him, and he referred to his own salary claims and the absence of officially agreed directors' fees or salaries.
On 2021-02-01, Farzin Ratan Karma emailed Asis Rose Dela Rosa stating that there would be no change in the work he assigned to her unless he instructed otherwise.

On 2021-02-24, after receiving an email from Helen Campos requesting payment of Farohar Enterprizes' share of Rose's salary, Farzin Ratan Karma responded that he was owed more by MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd than the monthly payment due from Farohar Enterprizes.

On 2020-07-17, at an extraordinary general meeting of MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd and MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd, directors including Chris Allix and Madadevan Lukshumayeh appointed Helen Campos as General Manager of both companies.

On 2019-11-08, an extraordinary general meeting of MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd and MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd was held at which Dr Theyvendran s/o Ramanathan was appointed a director of both companies.

On 2020-06-29, Helen Campos requisitioned an extraordinary general meeting of MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd and MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd to appoint Chris Allix as director and Madadevan Lukshumayeh as alternate director for Dr Theyvendran.

After the February 2022 rights share allotments, Farzin Ratan Karma's shareholding in MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd was reduced to 5% and his shareholding in MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd was reduced to 0.013%.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct and corporate arrangements, the plaintiff minority shareholder experienced commercially prejudicial treatment in relation to director remuneration, allocation of business and revenues among related entities, and rights issues affecting his shareholding in MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd and MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: In minority shareholder disputes, courts assess whether conduct departs from standards of fair dealing and violates conditions of fair play that shareholders are entitled to expect, having regard to the company constitution, any shareholder agreements, and objectively ascertainable expectations arising from the commercial relationship, with closer scrutiny where a company operates as a quasi-partnership.
Party_Details.Application: The plaintiff held 35% shareholdings and directorships in MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd and MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd, participated in a 2014 Directors' Agreement that fixed a lump-sum settlement for pre-2014 financial matters and set out revenue-sharing and remuneration structures, and later entered into a 2017 Waiver Agreement addressing recorded debts. He also used his personal company Farohar Enterprizes in arrangements such as Rose's Salary Agreement and received revenue allocations under the Directors' Agreement. Subsequent rights issues were notified to him with specific dates and amounts, and he chose not to subscribe, resulting in dilution of his shareholdings. These events frame the commercial context in which his expectations about remuneration, revenue allocation, and shareholding stability must be evaluated against the written agreements and corporate actions taken before the dispute.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court analysed whether MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd (MCCS) and MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd (MCAS) were quasi-partnerships, the propriety of Helen Campos' salary drawings, alleged diversion of business to HC Consultancy Pte Ltd (HCCS), and the rights issues, as well as the defendants' counterclaims. On quasi-partnership, the judge held that MCCS and MCAS were not quasi-partnerships. The companies were incorporated with Helen Campos as sole shareholder and director, and the plaintiff only became director, bank signatory, and shareholder in stages. The court found no core informal understandings about shareholding or roles at incorporation, and noted that Helen Campos effectively controlled who became shareholder and director, which weighed against a quasi-partnership. The plaintiff's continued employment at Ulysess until 2013 and the timing of his demands for remuneration when his income there declined further undermined his quasi-partnership narrative. Consequently, equitable considerations associated with quasi-partnerships did not apply, and the yardstick for unfairness was confined to legal rights and legitimate expectations from the constitution and agreements.

On salaries, the court treated the 2014 Directors' Agreement as binding. Clause 2(C) was interpreted as a full and final settlement of all business matters, including salary and dividends, for 2007-2013, precluding challenges to pre-2014 salary drawings. For post-2014 salaries, the court examined Clause 2(A)(iii) and the plaintiff's handwritten amendments. It found that the plaintiff had not proved that his amendments were mutually agreed or that they suspended Helen Campos' entitlement to a S$30,000 monthly salary. The absence of her countersignature next to the amendments, the wording of the annotations, and the plaintiff's own inconsistent explanations led the court to conclude that the original clause remained operative. Even assuming a technical inconsistency with the Directors' Agreement or the articles, the court held that a S$30,000 monthly salary was not commercially unfair given that the companies were essentially her professional practice, she generated the bulk of the billings, and her salary had remained constant since 2008 with reductions during her disqualification and the COVID-19 period. Any breach did not rise to oppression under s 216.

On alleged diversion of business and revenues to HCCS, the court held that Clause 2(C) of the Directors' Agreement also barred complaints about pre-2014 conduct. For post-2014 conduct, the court interpreted Clause 2(D) as a revenue-sharing mechanism allocating percentages of billings for new matters of existing and new clients among MCCS/MCAS, the directors' personal companies (HCCS and Farohar Enterprizes), and the other director. The court accepted Helen Campos' evidence that she implemented this by invoicing through HCCS and crediting the agreed share to MCCS, and that the phrase 'new matter of existing client' covered repeat work of the same type so long as it was a new item of work. The court found that the parties' disputes at the time focused on revenue allocation and drawings, not on share value or goodwill, and that the plaintiff himself used a similar invoicing pattern through Farohar Enterprizes from 2017 without objection. It concluded that the invoicing and allocation practice was within the scope of Clause 2(D) and not commercially unfair or oppressive.

On the rights issues, the court compared the facts with Over & Over Ltd v Bonvests Holdings Ltd. It found that MCCS and MCAS faced genuine cash flow constraints, exacerbated by the revenue-sharing arrangement, reduced business during COVID-19, and the companies' reliance on loans from Helen Campos and government support. Even excluding her salaries, average monthly cash balances were low or negative. The court noted that after the plaintiff obtained an injunction blocking the rights issue, Helen Campos extended a further loan to keep the companies operating, supporting the existence of a real funding need. The plaintiff received notice of the MCCS rights issue on 1 October 2021, attended a directors' meeting on 5 October 2021, and had 21 days before the 20 October 2021 EGM and until 22 October 2021 to subscribe for his entitlement, which the court considered a reasonable period to raise the required sum compared to Over & Over. The court rejected the contention that the rights issues were primarily designed to dilute the plaintiff's shareholding, noting that he himself sought more time to consider the proposal and that there was no quasi-partnership or legitimate expectation that his shareholding would remain constant. The pricing of the rights shares was linked to budgets reflecting the companies' financial position, and the court accepted that the directors believed the issues were necessary to raise capital. It therefore held that the rights issues were bona fide capital-raising exercises and did not amount to oppression.

On the counterclaims, the court considered Rose's Salary Agreement between MCCS and Farohar Enterprizes. It found that no written notice of termination was given and that Rose continued to perform work for Farohar Enterprizes after January 2021, based on her own evidence and the plaintiff's 1 February 2021 email instructing that her assignments would not change. The court concluded that the agreement remained in force and that Farohar Enterprizes' non-payment from January to July 2021 breached its payment obligation. The court treated the plaintiff's conduct in causing MCCS to enter into the agreement with his own company and then not ensuring payment as a breach of his fiduciary obligations as director, including the duty to act in the company's interests and to avoid conflicts. The court also addressed the plaintiff's use of MCCS funds for personal expenses and the effect of the Waiver Agreement, ultimately allowing parts of the defendants' counterclaim and rejecting others. Overall, the court dismissed the plaintiff's oppression claim and partially allowed the defendants' counterclaim, resulting in the conclusion that the plaintiff's claim was dismissed.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Helen Campos

Facts.Text: In 2003, Helen Campos was working at a law firm whose practice included providing corporate secretarial services.
By 2005, Helen Campos had left the law firm and became a shareholder and managing director of ELTICI Corporate Services Pte Ltd's corporate secretarial practice.
Around October 2006, after ELTICI Corporate Services Pte Ltd decided to sell its corporate secretarial practice, Helen Campos decided to leave that company.
On 2006-11-28, Helen Campos incorporated MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd as a corporate services company.
On 2006-11-28, Helen Campos became the sole director and sole shareholder of MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd upon its incorporation.
On 2008-01-29, Helen Campos incorporated MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd to provide accounting services.
On 2008-01-29, Helen Campos became the sole shareholder of MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd upon its incorporation.
By 2010, Ms Campos retained the remaining 65% shareholding of each company until October 2019, when she transferred one share in each to her son, Andrew Cherian Thomas.
On 19 February 2008, Mr Karma acquired a 35% shareholding in MCCS (35,000 shares) from Ms Campos.
On 21 April 2009, Mr Karma was appointed a director of MCAS and acquired a 35% shareholding (805 shares). He lost both his shareholding and directorship in MCAS on 23 April 2009.
On 17 September 2010, Mr Karma regained his 35% shareholding in MCAS.

On 23 December 2008, Ms Campos incorporated HC Consultancy Pte Ltd, intending to use it to collect revenue generated from her provision of nominee-director services and other services outside the purview of MCCS or MCAS' business.
On 23 April 2014, Ms Campos and Mr Karma entered into the Directors' Agreement, dated 1 January 2014, which provided for remuneration, a lump sum payment of S$350,000, and apportionment of billings.
The Directors' Agreement provided for payment of a lump sum of S$350,000 by Ms Campos to Mr Karma in settlement of any possible claims or liabilities between themselves, MCCS, MCAS, HCCS, or FEL.
From 2007, after the incorporation of MCCS, Ms Campos drew a salary of S$23,000 per month, which was increased to S$30,000 upon the incorporation of MCAS in 2008.

On 9 March 2017, Ms Campos was disqualified from acting as a director in or taking part in the management of a company pursuant to s 155A of the Companies Act because she had been a director in three companies that were struck off by the Registrar within a five-year period.
Ms Campos officially resigned from MCCS and MCAS in October 2019, having been granted several extensions of time to resign her directorships by ACRA.
On 8 October 2019, while still disqualified from holding directorships, Ms Campos transferred one share each in MCCS and MCAS to her son, Mr Thomas.
On 8 November 2019, Ms Campos convened an extraordinary general meeting to appoint Dr Theyvendran s/o Ramanathan as a director of MCCS and MCAS.

On 29 June 2020, Ms Campos requisitioned another EGM for the purposes of appointing Mr Allix as a director of MCCS and MCAS, and for Mr Lukshumayeh to be appointed as an alternate director for Dr Theyvendran.
On 17 July 2020, at the EGM, Mr Allix and Mr Lukshumayeh appointed Ms Campos as the General Manager of MCCS and MCAS.
Mr Lukshumayeh was subsequently appointed a director of MCCS and MCAS on 29 December 2020.
On 1 February 2021, Ms Campos was granted leave by the court to act as a director of MCCS and MCAS.
As of 2 November 2017, the accounts of MCCS and MCAS showed Mr Karma owing a total debt of S$546,015.76, and Ms Campos and Mr Karma entered into a Waiver Agreement on that date to waive the sum.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, given the incorporation structure, share transfers, remuneration practices, revenue allocation arrangements, and capital-raising steps implemented by Helen Campos before the dispute, her conduct towards the plaintiff as minority shareholder and co-director created a commercially prejudicial conflict over control, remuneration, and dilution of shareholding.
Party_Details.Rule: In assessing minority oppression, courts examine whether controlling shareholders or directors have exercised their powers in a manner that departs from fair dealing and violates legitimate expectations derived from the company constitution, shareholder agreements, and the commercial context, including how remuneration, related-party transactions, and capital-raising are structured and implemented.
Party_Details.Application: Helen Campos incorporated MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd and MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd as vehicles for her corporate secretarial and accounting practice, initially holding all shares and directorships. She later transferred minority stakes and directorships to Farzin Ratan Karma without requiring capital contributions from him, while retaining majority control. In 2014, she entered into a Directors' Agreement with him that settled pre-2014 financial matters and set out salary and revenue-sharing mechanisms involving HC Consultancy Pte Ltd and Farohar Enterprizes. She continued to draw a fixed monthly salary and implemented revenue allocations consistent with the written agreement. When the companies experienced cash constraints, including during the COVID-19 period, she arranged rights issues in 2021–2022 to raise capital, notified the plaintiff, and subscribed for shares herself, leading to an increase in her percentage shareholdings when he did not subscribe. These pre-dispute actions define the structural and financial environment in which the later dispute over oppression and dilution arose.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court considered whether Helen Campos' conduct as majority shareholder and director amounted to oppression of the plaintiff under s 216 of the Companies Act and whether she was liable on the counterclaims. On the quasi-partnership issue, the court held that MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd (MCCS) and MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd (MCAS) were not quasi-partnerships. They were incorporated by Helen Campos alone, and the plaintiff only became director, bank signatory, and shareholder later, without any clear pre-incorporation understanding about his role or shareholding. The court found that she effectively controlled entry and exit of shareholders and directors, including the temporary grant and revocation of the plaintiff's MCAS shareholding and directorship in 2009, which was inconsistent with a mutual trust-based quasi-partnership. The plaintiff's continued employment elsewhere and the timing of his demands for remuneration further supported the conclusion that the companies were her professional vehicles rather than joint ventures.

On remuneration, the court treated the 2014 Directors' Agreement as binding and interpreted Clause 2(C) as a full and final settlement of all pre-2014 financial matters, including salary and dividends, thereby barring challenges to her pre-2014 salary drawings. For post-2014 salaries, the court examined Clause 2(A)(iii) and the plaintiff's handwritten amendments. It found that the amendments were not shown to have been mutually agreed, noting the absence of her countersignature next to them and the ambiguous wording. Even if the amendments were operative, the court interpreted them as confirming agreement to a S$30,000 monthly salary subject to possible refinement in future employment contracts, not as suspending her entitlement. The court accepted that she had drawn a constant salary since 2008, that she generated most of the companies' billings, and that she reduced her drawings during her disqualification and the COVID-19 period. It concluded that her salary level and method of drawing it were not commercially unfair or oppressive.

On alleged diversion of business and revenues to HC Consultancy Pte Ltd (HCCS), the court held that Clause 2(C) of the Directors' Agreement precluded complaints about pre-2014 conduct. For post-2014 conduct, the court interpreted Clause 2(D) as a revenue-sharing framework that allowed each director to route 50% of billings for new matters of existing and new clients through their personal companies (HCCS for her, Farohar Enterprizes for the plaintiff), with specified percentages allocated to MCCS/MCAS and the other director. The court accepted her evidence that she implemented this by invoicing through HCCS and crediting the agreed share to MCCS, and that 'new matter of existing client' included repeat work of the same type so long as it was a new item of work. The court noted that the plaintiff himself used a similar invoicing pattern through Farohar Enterprizes from 2017 and had not objected at the time. It found no evidence that HCCS provided accounting services in place of MCAS. The court concluded that her invoicing and allocation practices were within the scope of the Directors' Agreement and did not constitute diversion of business in a way that was oppressive.

On the rights issues, the court evaluated whether they were bona fide capital-raising measures or a device to dilute the plaintiff's shareholding. It found that MCCS and MCAS faced genuine cash flow difficulties, partly due to the revenue-sharing arrangement and partly due to reduced business during COVID-19. The companies relied on government support and loans from Helen Campos, and she extended an additional loan after the plaintiff obtained an injunction blocking the rights issue. The court considered the timing and notice given: the plaintiff received notice of the MCCS rights issue on 1 October 2021, attended a directors' meeting on 5 October 2021, and had until 22 October 2021 to subscribe, which the court regarded as a reasonable period to raise the required funds. The rights issues were sized to raise approximately S$250,000 for MCCS and S$150,000 for MCAS, and the pricing was based on budgets reflecting the companies' financial position. The court distinguished the case from Over & Over Ltd v Bonvests Holdings Ltd, noting the absence of a quasi-partnership, the presence of a commercial funding need, and the more generous timeline. It held that the rights issues were bona fide and not oppressive, even though they resulted in dilution of the plaintiff's shareholding when he chose not to subscribe.

On the counterclaims, the court found that Rose's Salary Agreement between MCCS and Farohar Enterprizes remained in force from January to July 2021 because no written termination notice was given and Rose continued to perform work for Farohar Enterprizes, as evidenced by her testimony and the plaintiff's 1 February 2021 email. Farohar Enterprizes' non-payment of the agreed S$1,700 per month during that period breached the agreement. The court held that the plaintiff, as director of MCCS, breached his fiduciary duties by causing MCCS to enter into the agreement with his own company and then failing to ensure payment, thereby placing himself in a conflict and causing loss to MCCS. The court also examined his use of MCCS funds for personal expenses and the effect of the 2017 Waiver Agreement, concluding that some of the defendants' monetary claims were made out while others were not. Overall, the court dismissed the plaintiff's oppression claim against Helen Campos and the companies and partially allowed the defendants' counterclaim, leading to the conclusion that she was not liable for oppression.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd was incorporated on 2006-11-28 to provide corporate secretarial and general management consultancy services.
On 2006-11-28, MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd was incorporated with Helen Campos as its sole shareholder and director.
On 2006-12-12, Farzin Ratan Karma was appointed a director of MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd.
On 2007-06-09, Farzin Ratan Karma was appointed as a signatory for MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd's bank account with United Overseas Bank Limited.
On 2008-02-19, 35,000 shares in MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd were transferred from Helen Campos to Farzin Ratan Karma, resulting in his 35% shareholding and her 65% shareholding.

On 2014-04-23, MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd became a party to the Directors' Agreement signed by Helen Campos and Farzin Ratan Karma, which set out director remuneration, a S$350,000 settlement payment, and revenue-sharing arrangements involving HC Consultancy Pte Ltd and Farohar Enterprizes.

As of 2017-11-02, the accounts of MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd and MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd showed that Farzin Ratan Karma owed a total of S$546,015.76, which was later waived under a Waiver Agreement.

On 2017-11-02, MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd was a beneficiary of a Waiver Agreement between Helen Campos and Farzin Ratan Karma under which the recorded debt of S$546,015.76 owed by him to MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd and MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd was waived.

On 2020-01-10, MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd entered into Rose's Salary Agreement with Farohar Enterprizes, under which Asis Rose Dela Rosa would spend some of her working hours providing services to Farohar Enterprizes in exchange for a monthly payment of S$1,700 from Farohar Enterprizes.
Up to December 2020, Farohar Enterprizes paid MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd S$1,700 monthly under Rose's Salary Agreement.
From January 2021 to July 2021, Farohar Enterprizes did not pay MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd the S$1,700 monthly amount under Rose's Salary Agreement.
On 2019-11-08, an extraordinary general meeting was held to appoint Dr Theyvendran s/o Ramanathan as a director of MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd.

On 2020-07-17, an extraordinary general meeting was held at which Mr Allix and Mr Lukshumayeh were appointed as directors of MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd, and Ms Campos was appointed General Manager.
On 2020-12-29, Mr Lukshumayeh was appointed a director of MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd.
On 2021-10-20, an extraordinary general meeting of MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd passed a resolution authorising a rights issue.

In February 2022, MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd allotted shares under the rights issue; Farzin Ratan Karma did not take up the rights issue, and his shareholding in MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd fell from 35% to 5%.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the pre-dispute corporate actions and financial arrangements of MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd, including director appointments, revenue-sharing, related-party agreements, and a rights issue, created a neutral structural conflict over funding needs, director conduct, and the plaintiff's shareholding.
Party_Details.Rule: Corporate entities may raise capital, enter into related-party agreements, and allocate revenues in accordance with their constitutions and shareholder agreements, and disputes over such actions are evaluated by reference to compliance with those instruments and the commercial rationale for the transactions.
Party_Details.Application: MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd was incorporated with a single controlling shareholder and later admitted a minority shareholder-director. It became party to a Directors' Agreement that defined remuneration and revenue-sharing with related entities, and to a Waiver Agreement addressing recorded debts owed by the minority director. It also entered into Rose's Salary Agreement with the minority director's personal company and later implemented a rights issue to address cash flow constraints. These pre-dispute steps set the factual background for the later disagreement over whether the company's funding measures, dealings with the minority director's company, and allocation of revenues were consistent with its constitution and the agreed frameworks.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court treated MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd (MCCS) as a corporate defendant whose conduct was largely implemented through its directors, principally Helen Campos and, at times, the plaintiff and other directors. It found that MCCS was not a quasi-partnership but a company originally incorporated and controlled by Helen Campos. The court held that the 2014 Directors' Agreement and the 2017 Waiver Agreement were binding on the parties and on MCCS as a corporate party, and that Clause 2(C) of the Directors' Agreement barred challenges to pre-2014 financial matters, including salary and revenue allocations. The court accepted that MCCS implemented the revenue-sharing mechanism in Clause 2(D) by allowing billings for new matters of existing and new clients to be routed through HC Consultancy Pte Ltd and Farohar Enterprizes, with agreed percentages credited back to MCCS, and that this practice was consistent with the agreement.

Regarding Rose's Salary Agreement, the court found that MCCS entered into a valid contract with Farohar Enterprizes on 10 January 2020 for the provision of services by its employee, Asis Rose Dela Rosa, in exchange for a monthly payment of S$1,700. It held that the agreement remained in force from January to July 2021 because no written termination notice was given and Rose continued to perform work for Farohar Enterprizes. The court concluded that Farohar Enterprizes' non-payment during that period caused a loss to MCCS and that the plaintiff, as director, breached his fiduciary duties by causing MCCS to enter into the arrangement and then failing to ensure payment. MCCS was therefore entitled to recover the unpaid sums and related amounts such as maternity pay.

On the rights issue, the court found that MCCS faced genuine cash flow difficulties, including low or negative average monthly cash balances even when excluding Helen Campos' salaries, and reliance on her loans and government support. It accepted that the rights issue approved on 20 October 2021 was a bona fide attempt to raise capital rather than a device to oppress the plaintiff. The court noted that the plaintiff received notice and had a reasonable period to consider and subscribe for his entitlement but chose not to do so. It held that the resulting dilution of his shareholding did not amount to oppression by MCCS. Overall, the court dismissed the oppression claim against MCCS and allowed parts of its counterclaim relating to unpaid sums under Rose's Salary Agreement and the effect of the Waiver Agreement.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd was incorporated on 2008-01-29 to provide accounting services.
On 2008-01-29, MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd was incorporated.
On 2009-04-21, Farzin Ratan Karma was appointed a director of MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd.
On 2009-04-21, 805 shares in MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd were transferred to Farzin Ratan Karma, giving him a 35% shareholding.
On 2009-04-23, Farzin Ratan Karma lost both his shareholding and directorship in MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd.
On 2010-09-17, 805 shares in MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd were transferred to Farzin Ratan Karma, resulting in his 35% shareholding and Helen Campos' 65% shareholding.
On 2013-02-25, Farzin Ratan Karma was appointed again as a director of MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd.

On 2014-04-23, MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd became a party to the Directors' Agreement signed by Helen Campos and Farzin Ratan Karma, which set out director remuneration, a S$350,000 settlement payment, and revenue-sharing arrangements involving HC Consultancy Pte Ltd and Farohar Enterprizes.
As of 2017-11-02, the accounts of MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd showed that Farzin Ratan Karma owed the companies a total debt of S$546,015.76.

On 2017-11-02, Helen Campos and Farzin Ratan Karma entered into a Waiver Agreement to waive the recorded debt of S$546,015.76 owed by him to MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd and MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd.
On 2019-11-08, an extraordinary general meeting of MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd was held at which Dr Theyvendran s/o Ramanathan was appointed a director.

On 2020-07-17, an extraordinary general meeting was held at which Mr Allix and Mr Lukshumayeh were appointed as directors of MCCS and MCAS, and they appointed Ms Campos as the General Manager of MCCS and MCAS.
On 2020-12-29, Madadevan Lukshumayeh was appointed a director of MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd.

In February 2022, MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd allotted shares under the approved rights issue; Farzin Ratan Karma did not subscribe, and his shareholding fell to 0.013% while Helen Campos held 99.865% of the shareholding and Mr Allix held one share.

In February 2022, MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd allotted shares under the approved rights issue; Farzin Ratan Karma did not subscribe, and his shareholding was reduced to 0.013% while Helen Campos' shareholding increased to approximately 99.865% and Chris Allix held one share.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the pre-dispute governance and funding decisions of MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd, including director appointments, participation in the Directors' Agreement and Waiver Agreement, and a rights issue, formed a neutral corporate context for the later conflict over oppression and dilution of the plaintiff's shareholding.
Party_Details.Rule: A company's participation in shareholder agreements, debt waivers, and capital-raising measures is assessed against its constitution and the commercial rationale for such steps, and any later dispute over minority prejudice is evaluated in light of these pre-dispute corporate actions.
Party_Details.Application: MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd was incorporated and initially wholly owned by Helen Campos, later admitting the plaintiff as a 35% shareholder and director through share transfers. It became party to the 2014 Directors' Agreement, which defined remuneration and revenue-sharing, and to the 2017 Waiver Agreement, which addressed recorded debts owed by the plaintiff. In 2022, it implemented a rights issue to raise funds, which the plaintiff did not subscribe to, resulting in a significant reduction of his percentage shareholding. These steps, taken before the judgment, define the structural and financial setting in which the plaintiff's later complaints about oppression and dilution were raised.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court regarded MC Accounting Services Pte Ltd (MCAS) as a corporate defendant whose actions were carried out through its directors, primarily Helen Campos and, at times, the plaintiff and other directors. It held that MCAS, like MC Corporate Services Pte Ltd, was not a quasi-partnership but a company originally incorporated and controlled by Helen Campos. The court found that MCAS was bound by the 2014 Directors' Agreement and the 2017 Waiver Agreement, and that Clause 2(C) of the Directors' Agreement barred challenges to pre-2014 financial matters. It accepted that MCAS participated in the revenue-sharing mechanism under Clause 2(D), whereby billings for new matters of existing and new clients could be routed through HC Consultancy Pte Ltd and Farohar Enterprizes with agreed percentages credited back to MCAS.

The court considered MCAS's financial position and its participation in the rights issue approved in January 2022. It found that MCAS faced cash flow constraints, including negative average monthly cash balances even when excluding Helen Campos' salaries, and that the rights issue was a bona fide attempt to raise capital rather than a device to oppress the plaintiff. The plaintiff had the opportunity to subscribe but chose not to, resulting in dilution of his shareholding. The court held that this dilution did not amount to oppression by MCAS, particularly in the absence of a quasi-partnership and in light of the commercial need for funding. The court therefore dismissed the oppression claim against MCAS while allowing parts of the defendants' monetary counterclaims that related to the plaintiff's conduct affecting both companies.
